notes: Autonomous-loop iteration cap reached: the agent runs a bounded self-prompting loop, hits its max-iteration cap before fully succeeding, and resolves to ready cleanly rather than getting stuck in working. WHAT aider's loop IS (agent_supports:partial scope_note): aider's only autonomous self-prompting loop is the REFLECTION loop in coders/base_coder.py run_one() — after send_message, if the model's output triggers a follow-up (auto-lint failure, auto-test failure, or an edit/format error) aider auto-generates a reflected_message and re-prompts the model WITHOUT user intervention. It is hard-capped at max_reflections=3 (base_coder.py:101); on hitting the cap it stops with tool_warning('Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.') (base_coder.py:939-941) and returns from the turn. This is a genuine bounded self-prompting cap with a clean turn-end — the primitive this scenario tests. It is partial (not yes) because (1) the cap is a hardcoded constant with NO CLI/env/settings override (aider --help exposes no --max-reflections / --max-iterations), so a recording must DRIVE the loop to its cap via repeated auto-lint/auto-test failures rather than pre-lowering it, and (2) it is a reflection / error-recovery loop, not an open-ended goal-pursuit autonomous mode like claudecode's /goal — aider iterates only when its own output fails validation, so you cannot direct it toward an arbitrary success condition. SESSION IDENTITY (same as aider/self-correction-iteration and aider/auto-executed-tool-call): aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD, RawLineParser, core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/agent.go), so the daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session and no handoff/reconcile phase. Every event (transcript_new, presession_created, the state arc, pid_discovered, process_exited) lands on the one proc-<PID> row, so the turn arc, pid_bind and teardown all pin to session_birth via same_session_as. To avoid a transient proc-<PID> presession row stealing identity, session_birth anchors to recording start UNPINNED; turn_start (the first real working) establishes the bindable session and turn_end/teardown chain off session_birth's match. WHY this is ONE turn for aider (matches self-correction): io.user_input() writes the `#### ` history header exactly once per top-level prompt, BEFORE Coder.run_one; run_one's reflection loop sets reflected_message and calls send_message directly, never re-emitting a `#### ` header (base_coder.py:860/879/924-944). The parser treats every `> Tokens: …` line as assistant_message (NOT turn_done) so the turn stays open across the multiple capped model calls (parser.go:18-23,146-188); auto-lint/auto-test `> Running …` markers never change state away from working. So the whole capped reflection chain is one contiguous working span that settles to ready exactly once at the single turn end. SCOPE NOTE / irrlicht_observes:partial (cap-hit is INDISTINGUISHABLE from a normal turn-end): irrlicht observes the graceful-termination SHAPE (working → ready, no stuck working, no hang) but CANNOT discriminate 'loop hit its cap' from 'turn completed normally' — both terminate via the identical idle-flush turn_done. The cap-specific warning `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` IS written to the markdown transcript as a blockquote (tool_warning → append_chat_history(blockquote=True) in io.py) but the parser's errorRE matches only Error/Exception (not 'stopping'), so the line is treated as ordinary status blockquote and ignored (parser.go). The terminal working→ready is therefore synthesized by IdleFlush (aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter ~1500ms, parser.go:54,208-229) clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll — exactly as any normal aider turn ends. This is why the iteration-cap primitive is not surfaced as a distinct daemon signal; the spec asserts only the user-observable termination shape. (What would lift observes to yes: the aider adapter extracts the 'Only N reflections allowed, stopping.' blockquote into a first-class turn-end reason.) The KNOWN TIMING RISK shared with self-correction: if any single inter-reflection model call leaves the transcript silent >~1.5s, IdleFlush can fire a premature working→ready and the next `> Tokens:` reopens working — a flicker. The intermediate_working phase pins the no-flicker target; a fast local model avoids the flicker, a slow remote model can trip it (timing, not topology). If a real recording trips it, flag as a known timing limitation — do NOT widen the spec. NOT to be confused with token-quota-exhausted (a cost/budget limit) or turn-aborted-by-error (an unplanned provider error); this is a planned, count-based cap whose terminal signal is a clean turn-end. HOW runs differ: exact assistant wording, the auto-lint/auto-test output, per-turn token counts, and sub-second timing vary (model/OS-dependent); the working→ready edge lands with ~5s effective idle-flush latency (a timing delay, not a correctness gap). Stable structurally: one ready→working→ready arc on the one proc-<PID> session, a single process_exited, and a final ready — never stuck working, never waiting. NOTE: recording deferred — no aider auth in this environment (no provider key; headless aider hangs ~5min on provider OAuth; the rig precheck delegates auth to the user). This expected.jsonl is the DOCUMENTED TARGET the recording will validate against once auth is available.

text: Session appears in ready within 1s of agent launch
User prompt that fails auto-lint/auto-test → working; aider begins its bounded reflection loop, re-prompting itself toward a fix without user intervention (conceptual flow steps 1-2)

State stays continuously working across the capped reflection iterations — the loop self-prompts up to max_reflections=3 inside one turn; it must NOT flicker to ready or waiting between iterations and must NOT stick in working forever (the cap guarantees graceful termination)

Agent reaches its max-iteration cap, emits a terminal turn signal, and stops self-prompting → working → ready even though the goal was not fully met (conceptual flow steps 3-4; the cap-hit is indistinguishable from a normal turn-end — see observes:partial scope note); state never entered waiting
REPL torn down once after the turn → a single process_exited on the session; state immediately before teardown is ready
